intake.finding: Problem and intervention layer are clearly diagnosed: silent, permissionless access to motion/orientation sensors enables fingerprinting and tracking. WebKit (cdumez) opened w3c/deviceorientation#57 (2018-12-13) explicitly to add a requestPermission() API, citing a quantified privacy problem. Browser-layer permission gating is plausibly the correct fix. Ownership moved from the original Intel engineer (Raphael Kubo da Costa, 2019) to a Google security researcher (Tom Van Goethem) for the 2026 revival.
intake.limitations: No public intake/one-page diagnosis artifact; the ChromeStatus record is the de facto intake.

incubation.finding: Problem is real, important, and recurring, with strong independent research support. The solution shape (requestPermission() static methods on the event interfaces) has spec standing (merged via spec PR #68) and has shipped in WebKit/Safari since 2019. The alternative — a unified navigator.permissions.request() — never reached consensus (per Reilly Grant), so attaching permission to the event objects was the pragmatic path. Use cases (mobile games, VR/panorama, AR) are well established.
incubation.limitations: No public Chrome-side incubation/explainer; the explainer link in the record points to the spec section itself, not a Chrome explainer.

prototype.finding: A first Chrome prototype was pursued in a 2022 Intent to Prototype (Raphael Kubo da Costa) with a sound no-op-first plan (add the API returning current permission, add use counters, evaluate gating later) — but it set no milestones and stalled, leaving a ~4-year gap. The 2026 revival reuses the same design and now has a running M150 dev-trial implementation with WPT coverage referenced. No public friction logs or prototype decision log exist.
prototype.limitations: Implementation bug (crbug 947112) and Monorail issue 40094424 are auth-gated and could not be read; no public prototype/friction artifact; reason for the 2022 stall is undocumented.

developerTrials.finding: A developer trial is live at M150 behind the DeviceOrientationRequestPermission finch/flag (desktop + Android). However, the trial has a structural limitation: because the default permission remains ALLOW, requestPermission() is effectively a no-op and the permission-prompt UX path is not exercised, so the trial cannot reveal the prompt/over-prompting behavior that motivated the API. No public cohort, feedback instrument, or trial findings are evidenced.
developerTrials.limitations: No evidence of recruited cohort, representativeness, or published dev-trial readout; the no-op default sharply limits what the trial can learn.

wideReview.finding: Mixed. Positives: spec is mature (published W3C standard) and WebKit/Safari already shipped, so Chrome is closing an interop gap; API owners (Rick Byers, Mike Taylor) broadly endorsed matching WebKit. Concerns: TAG review is marked 'Not applicable' even though Alex Russell raised a substantive API-shape question (permission attached to event objects vs. a revived navigator.permissions.request()) and explicitly suggested the question belonged to the TAG; the recorded Mozilla signal 'In development' was disputed on-thread by API owner Mike Taylor ('I don't think a 7 year old bug w/ no activity counts as in development'); the actual Mozilla standards-position issue (#1428, opened 2026-06-29) remains 'Unscreened'; and web-develop signals are explicitly 'No signals'.
wideReview.limitations: Mozilla's formal position is unresolved as of cutoff; WebKit's position is only inferred from the 2019 ship and the cached 'Shipping on iOS' note, not a current dated statement.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial was conducted or planned for this milestone (origintrial=false; no OT stage). Because the M150 release ships no-op-by-default, no field experiment on the permission-prompt behavior is warranted yet. This is a genuine not-applicable at this phase, not an untested gap.
experiment.limitations: If/when Chrome moves to ASK-by-default, an origin trial or staged enforcement will become relevant; that phase has not commenced.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship was filed 2026-06-17 and approved by API owners (Chris Harrelson, Yoav Weiss) ~2026-07-15, just before cutoff, with DevTrial M150 and Ship M151 (desktop+Android). Spec and WPT are in place. However: security_review_status and privacy_review_status are both 'Pending' in the cached record; the design doc is not public (Yoav Weiss asked for it to be made public during review); two different design-doc IDs appear between the cached record and the intent thread; and the API-shape dissent raised during review was not routed to TAG.
prepareToShip.limitations: Approval-message bodies for the final LGTMs were partially truncated; formal security/privacy review artifacts are not public.

release.finding: The in-scope release event is the M150 dev trial. No Chrome DevRel blog, announcement, or coordinated dev-trial communication was found; the record's release-notes fields are explicitly not content-reviewed and not publish-ready (is_releasenotes_content_reviewed=false, is_releasenotes_publish_ready=false). MDN documents the static method. Given the documented iOS-13-era ecosystem breakage precedent, the absence of proactive comms around the eventual ASK-by-default transition is a forward-looking risk, though limited comms may be acceptable for a no-op dev trial.
release.limitations: No release-communication artifact found; cannot confirm internal launch-comms plan; the feature is not yet in stable so stable-release comms cannot exist yet.

adoption.finding: Not scorable for requestPermission() specifically: the API is in dev trial (M150) and no-op-by-default, and no exact use counter for requestPermission() exists in the mapped Chrome telemetry. A WebDX family counter is available — 'DeviceOrientationEvents' (bucket 102, web-feature-family scope, declared-family confidence) — which covers the broader device-orientation-events family rather than the permission API exactly. Across 571 timeline points it fired on 0.06043637% of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on 2026-07-21 (latest), 0.06094759% at the M150 launch checkpoint (2026-06-30, the dev-trial date), with a peak / first-observed-positive of 0.087135% on 2024-09-01 — i.e. a small, modestly declining baseline of underlying sensor-event usage that corroborates the relevance of the privacy intervention but cannot isolate requestPermission() uptake (day30/day90/day180 post-launch checkpoints are null; launch too recent). The recorded adoption plan ('sites with a valid sensor need, e.g. games, will call the API'; Chrome will eventually move to ASK-by-default) states no quantitative target. The iOS 13 precedent remains the strongest forward-looking signal: when Safari gated sensors by default it forced widespread requestPermission() adoption across panorama tools (Pannellum, krpano, Garden Gnome Software), 3D/creative frameworks (Babylon.js, p5.js), and generated large volumes of developer Q&A.
adoption.limitations: No exact requestPermission() counter (only a declared-family counter), so requestPermission() adoption — no-op in the dev trial — cannot be isolated; no unique-site, production-deployment, retention, task-success, or predeclared-target evidence; the iOS precedent is a different engine and a different (gate-by-default) phase than Chrome's current no-op trial.

support.finding: Not scorable for Chrome (not shipped to stable). Documented support risks exist for when gating activates: (a) w3c/deviceorientation#74 reports the requestPermission permission is stored per-session, which breaks shake-detection-style apps; (b) WebKit bug 221399 reports requestPermission is unavailable in cross-origin iframes even with feature policy (iOS 14) — a real interop/compat gap; (c) the iOS 13 permission change produced a large support burden across the ecosystem.
support.limitations: No Chrome support-volume data; iframe limitation may or may not apply to Chromium's implementation (unverified).

deprecation.finding: No deprecation or removal has commenced at M150: requestPermission() is an additive API and the default remains ALLOW. The longer-term plan to deprecate default access to window.ondevice{motion,orientation} and move to ASK-by-default is explicitly documented in both the 2022 and 2026 intents, but it is a future phase, not the current launch event.
deprecation.limitations: The eventual gating/deprecation is out of scope for this milestone; a separate review will be warranted when Chrome moves to ASK-by-default.

developerValue.finding: The developer value of a permission-gated sensor API is well-grounded: it gives legitimate sensor use cases (games, VR/panorama, AR) a cross-browser permission path and protects users from silent fingerprinting. However, web-develop demand was recorded as 'No signals' and the API ergonomics were contested on-thread (permission methods attached to event objects rather than a unified permission request), so developer value is real but partly asserted rather than evidenced.
developerValue.limitations: No Chrome-specific developer research or attributable partner evidence; 'No signals' was not mitigated.

endUserImpact.finding: Intended end-user benefit (control over silent motion/orientation sensor access; fingerprinting mitigation) is strong and evidence-backed, but only materializes once Chrome moves to ASK-by-default. At M150 the feature has zero end-user impact (no-op by default). The iOS precedent shows prompt-based gating also introduces UX friction and per-session re-prompting, a real cost.
endUserImpact.limitations: Current milestone has no measurable end-user impact; net benefit depends on the not-yet-commenced ASK-by-default phase.

adoption.finding: Unscored (not failed, not mixed): no exact counter for requestPermission() exists and the recorded adoption plan states no quantitative target, so there is no threshold to score against; with no predeclared target and no corroborating production/retention evidence the outcome stays unscored rather than substituting 'mixed' for an absent threshold. The only mapped Chrome telemetry is a WebDX declared-family counter — 'DeviceOrientationEvents' (bucket 102) — covering the broader device-orientation-events family, not requestPermission() exactly. It fired on 0.06043637% of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on 2026-07-21 (latest of 571 points), 0.06094759% at the M150 dev-trial checkpoint (2026-06-30), and a peak / first-observed 0.087135% on 2024-09-01 — a small, modestly declining baseline of underlying sensor-event usage (~0.06–0.09% of page loads) that corroborates the intervention's relevance but does not measure permission-API uptake (the API is no-op-by-default in the dev trial; day30/day90/day180 checkpoints are null). Unscored rather than failed because absence of exact data is inherent to a no-op dev trial of a family-countered capability, not evidence of failure.
adoption.limitations: No exact requestPermission() counter (only a declared-family counter); no unique-site, production-deployment, retention, task-success, user-benefit, or predeclared-target evidence; day30/day90/day180 post-launch checkpoints are null (launch 2026-06-30 too recent); will require re-scoring after stable ship and after any ASK-by-default transition.

interoperability.finding: Positive: defined in a mature W3C standard and already shipped in WebKit/Safari since 2019, so Chrome reduces a multi-year engine divergence; WPT tests exist for both motion and orientation requestPermission. Negative: Mozilla's position is unresolved (recorded 'In development' is disputed; standards-position issue is 'Unscreened'); a WebKit interop gap exists (requestPermission unavailable in cross-origin iframes despite feature policy); and Baseline is not yet achievable (only one shipping engine historically, Chrome only in dev trial).
interoperability.limitations: WPT actual pass/fail could not be verified (dashboard is JS-rendered); Mozilla position may resolve after cutoff.

implementationQuality.finding: Spec definition is precise and normative (transient-activation requirement, permission-state resolution, precision caps), and WPT is referenced for both events. Concerns: the actual prompting path is not exercised at M150 (no-op default); security and privacy review statuses are 'Pending' at Intent to Ship; and a prior implementation attempt stalled for years, so implementation continuity/maturity is uncertain.
implementationQuality.limitations: Implementation bugs are auth-gated; WPT results unverified; no public conformance/friction report.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: The underlying security problem is rigorously evidenced (quantified press coverage plus academic research) and the intent thread is candid. But the evidence ledger and comms have real defects: the Mozilla 'In development' signal is inaccurate and was disputed in-thread; the design doc is non-public (and inconsistently linked); TAG review was declined despite an API-shape question; security/privacy reviews are pending; web-dev signals are absent; and there is no Chrome DevRel release artifact for the dev trial.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Some review artifacts are private; cannot fully reconstruct the internal launch-comms plan.

support.finding: Not shipped to stable; no Chrome support volume or resolution data. Documented future support risks (per-session permission, cross-origin iframe gap, iOS-era breakage) are noted but unverified for Chromium. Unscored due to lack of post-ship evidence.
support.limitations: Will require re-scoring after stable ship.

overall.finding: A legitimate, long-overdue privacy/security alignment with Safari parity, a mature spec, and a pragmatic phased (no-op-first) rollout that deliberately avoids the iOS-13-style breakage. But it is advancing against 'No signals' from web developers, a disputed/unresolved Mozilla position, pending security and privacy reviews, a non-public design doc, API-shape dissent that was not routed to TAG, and no exact post-ship adoption evidence (dev trial only; a family-level DeviceOrientationEvents usage baseline of ~0.06% of measured Chrome page loads is available but does not measure requestPermission() uptake, and adoption is unscored with no declared target). A seven-year history with a stalled prior attempt and duplicate ChromeStatus entries reflects process friction. Strongest positives: problem validity, Safari parity, spec maturity; strongest concerns: review rigor, evidence accuracy, and communication.
overall.limitations: Overall must not hide that adoption/support/end-user-impact are unscored because the feature is not yet shipped to stable.

supports: Feature metadata, stages, planned DevTrial M150 / Ship M151 (desktop+Android), finch name DeviceOrientationRequestPermission, ff/safari/webdev views, security/privacy review 'Pending', TAG 'Not applicable', release notes not publish-ready, adoption plan (no-op first, later ASK-by-default)
sources.limitations: Cached record (accurate_as_of 2026-06-08); ff 'In development' signal disputed by later intent-thread evidence; is_released=false.

supports: Original 2018 API proposal by WebKit (Chris Dumez) to require permission for motion/orientation events
Security motivation citing sensor-exploitation research
Triggered spec PR #68
sources.limitations: Issue body only; issue now closed; full comment thread beyond WebKit confirmation not captured.

supports: Mozilla standards-position request filed 2026-06-29 by Tom Van Goethem
Status 'Unscreened'/'Open' as of retrieval
References Bugzilla 1536382
sources.limitations: No formal Mozilla decision yet; contradicts the cached ChromeStatus ff 'In development' signal.

supports: Intent to Ship filed 2026-06-17 by Tom Van Goethem
DevTrial M150 / Ship M151 (desktop+Android)
Mozilla 'In development' disputed by API owner Mike Taylor
Alex Russell API-shape dissent and TAG suggestion; Reilly Grant rationale (navigator.permissions.request never reached consensus)
Rick Byers +1 to match WebKit; Yoav Weiss flag that design doc is not public
Approved ~2026-07-15 by Chris Harrelson/Yoav Weiss

No-op-first plan with use counters

Links older ChromeStatus feature 5051845089689600
No milestones set; effort stalled

sources.publisher: W3C (Devices and Sensors / Web Applications Working Groups)
supports: Normative requestPermission() for DeviceOrientationEvent and DeviceMotionEvent
Section 7 requires express permission via requestPermission()
Precision caps (0.1 deg / deg/s / m/s^2) to mitigate fingerprinting
Changes log adds requestPermission(); SecureContext; Permissions API integration
Published standard (TR/orientation-event)
sources.limitations: Editor's Draft revision read; Automation section marked 'at risk'.

supports: Quantification of permissionless sensor access: 3,695 of top-100k sites tap mobile sensors (~1,200 for tracking; Wayfair/Priceline/Kayak named)
Cited by w3c#57 as the trigger
All major browsers allowed access without permission at the time
sources.limitations: Single 2018 press source; named-site list is historical.

supports: Underlying DeviceOrientation/Motion events are universally supported across engines (hence high compat risk when gating)
Safari on iOS supported 17.0+
sources.limitations: Tracks the events generally, not requestPermission support specifically; live page, no fixed observation date.

supports: iOS 13 turned off default sensor access and offered no setting to re-enable
Required code changes to call requestPermission()
Representative of broad ecosystem breakage (also krpano, Garden Gnome Software, Babylon.js, p5.js per adjacent search results)
Widespread developer friction: iOS 13 requires DeviceOrientationEvent.requestPermission()
Large volume of Q&A confirms the breakage was broad
sources.limitations: Community Q&A, not an authoritative spec; date approximate.

supports: Independent academic recommendation that sensors be guarded by permissions in Chrome and that browsers follow Tor/Safari's lead
sources.limitations: Read via search snippet only; full PDF not retrieved.

supports: Known limitation: requestPermission stores permission per session, making shake-event-detection apps impractical
sources.limitations: Read via search snippet only; full thread and resolution not captured.

supports: Interop/compat gap: requestPermission not available in cross-origin iframes even with feature policy (iOS 14+)
sources.limitations: Read via search snippet only; current status of the WebKit bug not verified.

supports: Reference documentation for the requestPermission() static method exists on MDN
sources.limitations: Referenced via search result; full MDN content not re-read.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status – WebFeature popularity timeline (Google)
supports: Mapped Chrome use counter for the device-orientation-events web feature: WebFeature 'DeviceOrientationEvents', bucket_id 102, web-feature-family scope, declared-family confidence (per web-features-mappings chrome-use-counters and ChromeStatus web_feature normalization)
Timeline of 571 points; counter fired on 0.06043637% of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on 2026-07-21 (latest)
At the M150 launch checkpoint (2026-06-30, the dev-trial date) the family counter fired on 0.06094759% of measured page loads; day30/day90/day180 post-launch checkpoints are null

Peak / first-observed-positive 0.087135% on 2024-09-01; baseline of underlying sensor-event usage is small and modestly declining (~0.06–0.09% of page loads), corroborating the relevance of the privacy intervention
No exact requestPermission() counter is mapped; this family counter does not measure requestPermission() adoption
sources.limitations: Declared-family (web-feature-family) counter, not an exact requestPermission() adoption counter; covers the broader DeviceOrientationEvents behavior that is universally supported and predates requestPermission(), so it cannot isolate requestPermission() uptake — which is no-op-by-default in the M150 dev trial; the counter may fire on detection/probing; numerator is measured Chrome page loads on which the counter fired, not unique sites, developers, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit; UMA reflects participating Chrome clients (enterprise/opt-out populations may be underrepresented); zero/missing/delayed points are not proof of zero real-world use.

successes: Security/privacy rationale is rigorously evidenced (quantified 2018 press coverage plus NDSS/PoPETS/IEEE research) rather than asserted.
Chrome is closing a multi-year engine divergence by aligning with a spec WebKit/Safari has shipped since 2019.
Phased no-op-first rollout deliberately avoids the iOS-13-style web breakage, informed directly by that precedent.
An API owner (Mike Taylor) candidly flagged the inaccurate Mozilla 'In development' signal during review rather than letting it stand.
Spec is precise and normative, including transient-activation and precision-cap mitigations for fingerprinting.
failures: Approximately seven-year delay with a stalled 2022 prototype attempt and no public postmortem explaining the stall.
Duplicate/competing ChromeStatus entries (5915984063889408 and 5051845089689600) for the same underlying capability, complicating tracking.
Mozilla position recorded as 'In development' on the strength of a dormant 7-year-old bug; disputed on-thread and still 'Unscreened' at the real standards-position issue.
TAG review declined ('Not applicable') despite a substantive API-shape design question raised during the intent.
Design doc is non-public (flagged by Yoav Weiss) and is inconsistently linked between the cached record and the intent thread.
Security and privacy review statuses are 'Pending' at Intent to Ship.
No Chrome DevRel/release artifact for the dev trial; release notes explicitly not content-reviewed or publish-ready.
Web-develop signals recorded as 'No signals' without any mitigation or attributable research.
counterfactuals: If the 2022 Intent to Prototype had set milestones and a follow-up owner, the API could have shipped ~4 years earlier, shortening the Chrome/Safari interop gap and the window of continued silent sensor access.

If a TAG review had been requested instead of 'Not applicable', the API-shape concern (permission on event objects vs a unified navigator.permissions.request()) could have been adjudicated before Chrome committed to the WebKit-compatible shape.
If the Mozilla position had been verified against a current primary source before Intent to Ship, the interop story would have been accurate rather than overstated as 'In development'.
If a Chrome DevRel announcement plus MDN/sample had launched with the dev trial, the ecosystem would be better prepared for the eventual ASK-by-default transition that broke sites on iOS 13.
If the design doc had been public, reviewers (and the ecosystem) could have evaluated the no-op-first vs ASK-by-default transition plan on its merits.
skillImprovements: Phase 04 (wide review): add a rule to verify every browser standards-position against a current, dated primary source and to flag any position older than a threshold (e.g. 24 months) or derived from a dormant bug as stale/unknown — never infer 'in development' from a ChromeStatus field.
Phase 06 (prepare to ship): add a rule to flag or block Intent to Ship when security_review_status or privacy_review_status is 'Pending'.

Phase 04: add a rule that when any reviewer raises an API-design/shape question during an intent, a TAG-review decision (not 'Not applicable') is required unless the shape is verbatim mandated by a CR/REC spec.

Phase 00 (intake/classification): add a duplicate-detection step that searches for existing ChromeStatus entries for the same capability and reconciles or marks them superseded before creating a new one.
Phase 03/07: require a public release-communication artifact (blog/MDN/sample) for any capability with a known ecosystem-breakage precedent, even for a dev trial.
Phase 06: add an evidence-ledger reconciliation check so the design doc linked in ChromeStatus matches the design doc referenced in the intent thread.

Add a regression eval case: a long-dormant, revived, spec-mandated security feature with Safari parity, 'No signals' from web devs, and an unresolved 3rd-engine position — to test that the reviewer scores it mixed (not success from parity/shipment alone) and demands current position verification.

Phase 08 (adoption/outcomes): before recording 'no Chrome usage telemetry exists,' always attempt to map the feature's web_feature to a WebDX use counter (exact, family/property, origin-trial, or multiple) via the web-features-mappings chrome-use-counters and the ChromeStatus WebFeature timeline, and record the mapping scope/confidence; a family/property counter is usage evidence for the broader family but is not exact feature adoption and must not be reported as 'no data'.

missingEvidence: Feature is in dev trial (M150), not shipped to stable; end-user-impact and support outcomes are inherently unscored at this milestone, and adoption is unscored because no exact requestPermission() counter exists and no quantitative target was declared.

No exact Chrome use counter for requestPermission(): the only mapped telemetry is a declared-family counter (DeviceOrientationEvents, bucket 102) that cannot isolate the permission API; consequently there is no unique-site, production-deployment, retention, or task-success adoption evidence, and the day30/day90/day180 post-launch checkpoints are null (launch 2026-06-30 is too recent).
WPT actual pass/fail results could not be verified (wpt.fyi dashboard is JS-rendered and returned no test data via the reader).
Implementation detail bugs (crbug 947112, Monorail 40094424) are auth-gated and unreadable.
Design doc(s) are non-public; content could not be assessed (two different doc IDs appear).
Mozilla standards position is 'Unscreened' as of cutoff; the 3rd-engine interop story is unresolved.
No public dev-trial cohort, feedback report, or Chrome release-communication artifact was found.
The older related ChromeStatus entry (5051845089689600) repeatedly returned HTTP 500 via the reader and could not be read directly (its 2022 Intent to Prototype thread stands in as proxy).
